## Quantitative Calibration {#sec-calibration}

This section maps the model parameters to the AI infrastructure sector using publicly available data from company filings, industry reports, and the scaling laws literature.
The calibration is *stylized*: the goal is to discipline the model's quantitative magnitudes and illustrate cross-sectional heterogeneity, not to structurally estimate firm-level primitives or test the model empirically.
Three caveats should be noted at the outset.
First, the Leland default framework is an analytically tractable approximation to financial distress risk; it fits public-debt issuers better than VC-backed labs, though the qualitative predictions apply across financing structures (see @sec-discussion).
Second, the training fractions $\hat{\phi}$ are inferred from incomplete data and carry substantial uncertainty ($\pm 0.10$).
Third, the scaling exponent $\eta$ is inferred from the neural scaling laws literature through a non-trivial mapping (see below).
The calibration proceeds in four steps: I first set the demand process parameters, then the technology and cost parameters, then the endogenous arrival rate parameters, and finally the financial parameters.

@tbl-param-mapping summarizes the calibration status of each parameter.

| Parameter | Baseline | Target / Source | Status |
|:----------|:--------:|:----------------|:------:|
| $\mu_L$ | 0.01 | Cloud computing baseline growth | Chosen for discipline |
| $\mu_H$ | 0.06 | Upper-range AI revenue growth projections | Chosen for discipline |
| $\sigma_L$ | 0.25 | Quarterly revenue volatility, cloud providers | Inferred |
| $\sigma_H$ | 0.30 | Elevated uncertainty under transformative AI | Chosen for discipline |
| $r$ | 0.12 | WACC, representative AI infrastructure firm | Inferred from Damodaran |
| $\lambda_0$ | 0.05 | Rest-of-world AI progress | Chosen for discipline |
| $\xi$ | 0 | Endogenous training contribution (baseline: exogenous) | Chosen |
| $\eta$ | 0.07 | Inferred from Kaplan et al. (2020), Hoffmann et al. (2022) scaling laws | Inferred |
| $\alpha$ | 0.40 | GPU utilization rates, diminishing marginal compute value | Inferred |
| $\gamma$ | 1.50 | Power/supply-chain bottlenecks, site prep costs | Chosen for discipline |
| $c$ | 1.00 | Normalization | --- |
| $\delta$ | 0.03 | Power, cooling, maintenance (flow cost only) | Inferred from industry |
| $c_d$ | 0.05 | Cost of debt for AI infrastructure firms | Inferred |
| $b$ | 0.30 | Bankruptcy cost fraction | Standard (Leland 1994) |

: Parameter calibration status. "Directly observed" = taken from empirical estimates; "Inferred" = backed out from observable proxies; "Chosen for discipline" = selected to generate plausible magnitudes without a precise empirical target. {#tbl-param-mapping tbl-colwidths="[14,10,42,18]"}

### Demand Process

The demand process parameters govern the drift and volatility of AI compute demand under each regime.
In the low regime ($L$), I set $\mu_L = 0.01$ and $\sigma_L = 0.25$, reflecting the modest growth and moderate uncertainty that characterizes the current AI services market outside of frontier model training; the low drift captures baseline growth in cloud computing revenue, while the volatility reflects demand uncertainty for inference services.
In the high regime ($H$), I set $\mu_H = 0.06$ and $\sigma_H = 0.30$, reflecting the substantially higher growth and elevated volatility that would characterize a world in which transformative AI capabilities generate massive new demand; the growth rate is calibrated to match the upper range of AI revenue growth projections from industry analysts.

The exogenous component of the arrival rate is $\lambda_0 = 0.05$ per year, reflecting rest-of-world AI progress (algorithmic advances, academic research, open-source contributions) that occurs independently of the modeled firms' investments.
The total effective arrival rate $\tilde{\lambda}$ combines this baseline with the endogenous contribution from firms' training compute (@eq-endogenous-lambda).
Under the baseline calibration with $\xi = 0$ (exogenous model), the total effective rate $\tilde{\lambda} = 0.10$ per year implies an expected time to regime switch of 10 years.
The baseline represents a moderate prior, intended to approximate the marginal investor's belief rather than any particular executive's view.
It is significantly more conservative than the beliefs implied by @amodei2024machines ($\lambda \approx 0.30$--$0.50$) or @hassabis2025breakthroughs ($\lambda \approx 0.10$--$0.15$), and the dispersion across these views is precisely what generates the cross-sectional variation in investment behavior analyzed in @sec-valuation.

I set the discount rate $r = 0.12$, the risk-adjusted WACC for a representative AI infrastructure firm.
As discussed in @sec-model, $r$ is the WACC and $\mu_s$ is the risk-adjusted (certainty-equivalent) growth rate; all valuation uses this reduced-form framework throughout.
The baseline exceeds typical corporate WACCs because AI infrastructure investments carry substantial technology risk and the firms are typically high-growth with elevated cost of equity.
The analytical results and figures in @sec-model use $r = 0.12$ throughout; these illustrate model properties at a representative WACC and do not correspond to any specific firm archetype.
The firm-specific WACCs in @tbl-firms (ranging from 0.10 to 0.18) enter the sensitivity analysis.

### Technology Parameters

The revenue elasticity $\alpha = 0.40$ governs diminishing returns to capacity in revenue generation, entering both the L-regime inference contest (@eq-contest-L) and the H-regime training contest (@eq-contest-H).
Doubling the relevant capacity measure increases revenue by approximately $2^{0.4} \approx 1.32$ times---a 32\% increase rather than 100\%---reflecting the empirical observation that GPU utilization rates decline as capacity grows and that the marginal value of additional compute diminishes as models approach capability frontiers.
The cost function $I(K) = cK^\gamma$ with convexity $\gamma = 1.50$ captures increasing marginal costs from power constraints, supply chain bottlenecks (particularly for advanced GPUs), and site preparation costs; I normalize the unit cost $c = 1.0$, so all monetary values are interpreted relative to this normalization.

The operating cost rate $\delta = 0.03$ represents the ongoing cost of maintaining installed capacity---power, cooling, maintenance, and personnel---expressed as a fraction of capacity per year, distinct from both accounting depreciation and economic depreciation of GPU hardware.
Accounting depreciation for data center equipment typically implies a 3--5 year useful life (20--33\% per year), and power costs alone can reach \$0.05--0.10/kWh at hyperscale.
In the model, the *capital* cost of hardware is captured in $cK^\gamma$; $\delta$ captures only the *operating* flow cost.
Economic depreciation through technological obsolescence (e.g., H100$\to$B200 transitions) would justify a higher effective $\delta$, making the baseline conservative.
The model is relatively insensitive to this parameter: @tbl-elasticities reports elasticities of $+2.2$ for the trigger and $+2.0$ for capacity, among the smallest of all parameters.
At $\delta = 0.10$, implied beliefs shift modestly but qualitative results are unchanged; a higher $\delta$ strengthens the paper's conclusions by making the AI investment dilemma more asymmetric, because higher operating costs amplify losses from overinvestment relative to the opportunity cost of underinvestment.

### Endogenous Arrival Rate Parameters

The scaling exponent $\eta = 0.07$ governs diminishing returns in the contribution of training compute to AI progress: doubling training compute raises the arrival rate contribution by only $2^{0.07} - 1 \approx 5\%$.
This value is *inferred* from the neural scaling laws literature, not directly observed.
@kaplan2020scaling and @hoffmann2022training report that model loss (cross-entropy) decreases as a power law of training compute, with exponents in the range 0.05--0.10 depending on the model family and data regime.
The mapping from loss reduction to regime-switch arrival rate involves two non-trivial steps: (i) translating a decrease in loss into an improvement in model capability (which depends on the relationship between loss and economically relevant performance metrics), and (ii) translating capability improvement into a marginal increase in the probability of the economy transitioning to the high-demand regime (which depends on adoption thresholds, complementary investments, and institutional readiness).
Neither step has a well-established empirical basis.
I use $\eta = 0.07$ as a plausible central value from the scaling law range, interpreting it as the elasticity of AI progress with respect to training compute, but the mapping from loss-compute power laws to arrival-rate elasticities is inherently uncertain and represents one of the calibration's weakest links.
The model is quantitatively robust to this parameter: with $\eta = 0.07$ and the baseline $\xi$, the endogenous channel is modest (doubling one firm's training raises $\tilde{\lambda}$ by approximately 5\%), so the exogenous baseline $\lambda_0$ dominates the arrival rate for most firm-level decisions.
The firm contribution scaling parameter $\xi$ converts the modeled firms' training compute into arrival rate units.
The baseline $\xi = 0$ corresponds to the exogenous-$\lambda$ model; for the endogenous analysis, I calibrate $\xi$ so that the industry's current training compute ($\sum \phi_i K_i$ across the four stylized firms) contributes approximately half of the total arrival rate, yielding $\tilde{\lambda} \approx 2\lambda_0$.
This 50\% split reflects the observation that frontier AI labs collectively conduct the majority of the world's largest training runs, but that academic research, open-source projects (e.g., Llama, Mistral), and international competitors (e.g., DeepSeek) contribute substantially to overall progress.
The choice is inherently uncertain; varying the endogenous share from 25\% to 75\% shifts the quantitative importance of the training externality but preserves all qualitative results (see Appendix F on exogenous vs. endogenous $\lambda$ robustness). With $\eta = 0.07$, the endogenous channel is quantitatively modest: doubling a single firm's training compute raises its contribution to $\tilde{\lambda}$ by only $2^{0.07} - 1 \approx 5\%$, so the model effectively reduces to the exogenous-$\lambda$ case for most firm-level decisions.

### Stylized Firm Archetypes

I construct four stylized firm archetypes representing the major categories of AI infrastructure investors.
These archetypes are designed to span the relevant cross-sectional variation in investment intensity, leverage, and training allocation; they are *illustrative composites*, not structural estimates of any specific company's parameters.

| Parameter | Frontier Lab | Platform | Hyperscaler | Compute Racer |
|:----------|:------------|:---------|:------------|:--------------|
| Archetype | Anthropic | OpenAI | Google | xAI |
| Revenue 2024 (\$B) | 0.9 | 4.0 | 40.0 | 0.5 |
| Revenue 2025 (\$B) | 3.0 | 12.0 | 55.0 | 2.0 |
| CapEx 2025 (\$B) | 6.0 | 15.0 | 60.0 | 10.0 |
| CapEx / Revenue | 2.00 | 1.25 | 1.09 | 5.00 |
| Leverage | 0.20 | 0.30 | 0.10 | 0.25 |
| WACC | 0.15 | 0.14 | 0.10 | 0.18 |
| Training fraction $\hat{\phi}$ | 0.60 | 0.55 | 0.40 | 0.80 |

: Stylized firm parameters calibrated from public filings and analyst reports. Training fractions $\hat{\phi}$ are estimated from industry reports on compute allocation. {#tbl-firms tbl-colwidths="[24,15,15,15,15]"}

The figures in @tbl-firms are approximate composites derived from public filings (10-K, 10-Q), earnings call guidance, and analyst estimates, rounded for expositional clarity.
For privately held firms (the Anthropic-like and xAI-like archetypes), revenue figures are based on press reports and analyst estimates and are inherently less reliable than publicly disclosed figures; they should be treated as order-of-magnitude estimates.
I cross-referenced multiple sources where available (e.g., Bloomberg, S\&P Capital IQ, Damodaran industry data; see @tbl-sources).
The stylized firms are designed to capture salient features of each category rather than to replicate any single company's financials precisely.
The qualitative results are robust to moderate perturbation of these inputs: @tbl-elasticities shows that the model's comparative statics are well-behaved across the calibration range.
For the Google-like archetype, the relevant revenue concept is Google Cloud revenue (growing 25--35\% annually), not Alphabet's total revenue.
The \$40--55B figures in @tbl-firms approximate Google Cloud's AI-related infrastructure spending and cloud revenue; using Alphabet-wide revenue (\$350B+) would dramatically reduce the CapEx/Revenue ratio and understate Google's AI investment intensity.
For the xAI-like archetype, revenue reflects standalone AI-related revenue (Grok API and subscription revenue); xAI's March 2025 merger with the X social media platform complicates revenue attribution, but the AI-specific figures best match the model's scope.
The same sector-specific revenue concept applies to all archetypes: revenue from AI-related activities, not diversified corporate totals.

The training fraction $\hat{\phi}$ estimates reflect the approximate share of compute allocated to model training versus inference serving.
These estimates are inherently uncertain, as firms do not publicly disclose precise training-inference breakdowns.
The values in @tbl-firms are inferred from multiple sources: @amodei2025training describes the allocation constraint and suggests approximately 50\% for frontier labs as "a stylized fact"; analyst reports from Bernstein, Morgan Stanley, and Coatue estimate that inference currently accounts for 60--80\% of cloud GPU demand at hyperscalers; and GPU utilization data from cloud provider dashboards suggest that dedicated training clusters run near-continuously while inference capacity has more variable utilization.
The xAI-like compute racer allocates the most to training ($\hat{\phi} = 0.80$), consistent with its strategy of dedicating all owned compute (the Colossus supercluster) to frontier model training while outsourcing inference to cloud providers [@musk2026colossus].
The Google-like hyperscaler allocates the least ($\hat{\phi} = 0.40$), reflecting its need to serve a large existing inference workload.
The Anthropic-like and OpenAI-like archetypes fall between these extremes.
These estimates carry substantial uncertainty---plausibly $\pm 0.10$ for each archetype---and readers should treat them as order-of-magnitude discipline rather than precise moments for structural identification.
The cross-sectional variation ($\hat{\phi}$ ranging from 0.40 to 0.80) is more informative than any individual point estimate, and the qualitative results are robust to moderate perturbation of these values (@tbl-elasticities).

@fig-firm-comparison provides a visual summary of the calibration targets.
Panel (a) shows the capital expenditure to revenue ratio for each stylized firm.
All four firms have investment intensities well above the unity threshold (dotted line), confirming that each is investing far ahead of current demand.
The xAI-like compute racer has by far the highest ratio (5.0$\times$), reflecting its extreme invest-first strategy of building massive training clusters before monetization; the Anthropic-like frontier lab is the next most aggressive at 2.0$\times$.
Panel (b) plots each firm in revenue-growth versus leverage space, revealing substantial dispersion: the xAI-like firm combines the highest revenue growth (4.0$\times$) with moderate leverage, while the Google-like hyperscaler combines modest growth with the lowest leverage.
This dispersion in financial characteristics drives the heterogeneous investment behavior analyzed in @sec-valuation.

![Stylized firm comparison. (a) Capital expenditure to revenue ratio (2025). All firms invest above the 1.0x threshold (dotted line), indicating investment ahead of current demand; the xAI-like compute racer is a pronounced outlier at 5.0$\times$. (b) Revenue growth (2024--2025) versus leverage ratio. The four firm archetypes occupy distinct positions in this space, reflecting different investment strategies and financial structures.](figures/fig_firm_comparison.pdf){#fig-firm-comparison}

Several patterns emerge from @tbl-firms.
First, all firms have capital expenditure exceeding revenue---investment intensities above 100\%---which is historically unusual and indicates that these firms are investing far ahead of current demand.
Second, the xAI-like compute racer has the most extreme investment intensity (5.0$\times$), reflecting a strategy of scaling compute capacity before monetization; the Anthropic-like frontier lab is the next most aggressive.
Third, leverage varies across archetypes: the hyperscaler funds investment primarily from internal cash flows ($\ell = 0.10$), while the OpenAI-like platform carries moderate debt from credit facilities ($\ell = 0.30$).
Fourth, training fractions vary by a factor of two across firm types (0.40 to 0.80), reflecting fundamentally different business models and beliefs about where the returns to AI compute investment will be highest.

### Baseline Results

Under the baseline calibration, the single-firm model with training-inference allocation produces an investment trigger $X^* \approx 0.027$, capacity $K^* \approx 0.055$, and training fraction $\phi^* \approx 0.70$, where the effective revenue coefficient $A_{\text{eff}}$ (@eq-a-eff) combines L-regime inference value and H-regime training value.
The duopoly equilibrium with zero leverage produces a follower policy of $X_F \approx 9.37$, $K_F \approx 17.63$, and $\phi_F \approx 0.70$, with the leader preempting at $X_P \approx 0.055$ and the monopolist trigger at $X_L^{\text{mono}} \approx 0.114$.

Strategic interaction accelerates investment (the preemption trigger $X_P \approx 0.055 < X_H^* \approx 0.114$) but reduces capacity per firm relative to the monopolist benchmark.
Both firms optimally allocate approximately 70\% to training ($\phi^* \approx 0.70$), reflecting the high value of the H-regime continuation payoff at the baseline arrival rate $\tilde{\lambda} = 0.10$; the optimal training fraction is determined by the balance between L-regime inference value and H-regime training value and is independent of the competitive position in the exogenous-$\lambda$ model.

### Sensitivity Analysis

The following comparative statics are robust across the calibration range; @tbl-elasticities in the Appendix reports elasticities at the baseline.
The investment trigger, capacity, and training fraction are sensitive to several key parameters.
Higher volatility $\sigma_H$ increases the option value of waiting, raising the investment trigger---the standard real options result that uncertainty raises the value of flexibility; for AI infrastructure, this means that higher uncertainty about future demand justifies more cautious investment timing, even as firms rush to build.
A higher discount rate $r$ reduces the present value of future cash flows, raising the trigger and reducing optimal capacity; the wide variation in WACC across AI firms (10\%--18\% in our calibration) implies substantially different investment policies even for firms with identical beliefs about $\lambda$.
Higher $\tilde{\lambda}$ increases the weight on H-regime revenue in $A_{\text{eff}}$ (@eq-a-eff), raising the effective revenue coefficient, lowering the investment trigger, and shifting the optimal training fraction $\phi^*$ upward as the expected payoff from training quality increases; the net effect is that higher $\tilde{\lambda}$ leads to earlier investment, larger capacity, and a higher share of training---consistent with the observation that more optimistic firms invest more aggressively and allocate more compute to frontier model development.
Finally, lower revenue elasticity $\alpha$ (stronger diminishing returns) reduces optimal capacity and raises the trigger; this parameter is crucial for the contest function specification, as lower $\alpha$ reduces the benefit of capacity dominance in both regimes, weakening the preemption incentive.
